did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used two mobile phone-powered AI instruments: a new 7-class AI algorithm and the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) AI algorithm, to diagnose and manage pigmented skin cancer. The AI models performed classification of pigmented lesions (diagnostic trial) and management decisions (management trial) on dermoscopy images, with results compared against human clinicians.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is explicitly titled and described as a 'multicentre, prospective, diagnostic, clinical trial' (Chunk 0, Chunk 1). It reports original experimental data from a clinical study involving 172 suspicious pigmented lesions in a diagnostic trial and 5696 pigmented lesions in a management trial, comparing the performance of mobile phone-powered AI algorithms against human clinicians. The paper includes Methods, Findings, and Interpretation sections typical of primary research, not a review of prior literature.

title: Comparison of humans versus mobile phone-powered artificial intelligence for the diagnosis and management of pigmented skin cancer in secondary care: a multicentre, prospective, diagnostic, clinical trial
brief_summary: This multicentre, prospective, diagnostic clinical trial evaluated the equivalence of mobile phone-powered artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms against human clinicians for diagnosing and managing pigmented skin cancer in secondary care. The study compared a new 7-class AI algorithm and the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) AI algorithm against specialist and novice clinicians using dermoscopy images from 172 suspicious lesions in a diagnostic trial and 5696 lesions in a management trial. The 7-class AI algorithm demonstrated diagnostic accuracy equivalent to specialists and significantly superior to novices, while the ISIC AI algorithm was inferior to specialists but superior to novices. In management decisions, the 7-class AI was significantly inferior to specialists but showed mixed performance compared to novices depending on the scenario. The study concludes that while mobile phone-powered AI is accurate for diagnosis, its application for management decisions requires careful execution, highlighting the need for caution when extrapolating experimental AI results to real-world clinical practice.

all_task_types.target: Seven-class diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic naevus, Basal cell carcinoma, AK/IEC, Benign keratotic, Benign vascular, Dermatofibroma) and management decisions (dismissal, biopsy, or monitoring).
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates a 7-class AI algorithm and the ISIC AI algorithm for the categorical classification of pigmented skin lesions into seven specific diagnostic classes. The primary endpoint was a non-inferiority comparison of the correct categorical classification of the seven possible diagnoses. Additionally, the management trial involved classifying lesions into management decisions (dismissal, biopsy, or 3-month monitoring) based on the diagnostic classification.

datasets_used.name: Diagnostic Trial Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective cohort of 124 patients from two tertiary referral centres in Australia (Sydney) and Austria (Vienna) undergoing routine excision or biopsy of suspicious pigmented skin lesions. Images were taken using a mobile phone-powered dermoscopic attachment (MoleScope II).
classes_used: Pigmented actinic keratosis or intraepithelial carcinoma (AK/IEC)

use.testing: Used as the primary index test cohort to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the 7-class AI algorithm and the ISIC AI algorithm against specialist and novice clinicians.

datasets_used.name: Management Trial Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective cohort of 66 high-risk patients from the same tertiary referral centres in Australia and Austria who had baseline total-body photographs taken within 1–4 years of examination. Lesions were assessed for management decisions (dismissal, biopsy, or monitoring).

use.testing: Used to evaluate the management decision accuracy of the 7-class management AI algorithms (1, 2, and 3) and the ISIC AI algorithm against specialist and novice clinicians.

datasets_used.name: MetaOptima Technology Database
datasets_used.description: A proprietary database of expert dermoscopy images of skin lesions taken from mobile phones using several dermoscope attachments, used to develop the 7-class diagnosis AI algorithm.
classes_used: Pigmented actinic keratosis or intraepithelial carcinoma

use.training: Used to develop the 7-class diagnosis AI algorithm.
use.other: Source of the algorithm used in the clinical trial.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: The dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 Challenge, used to train and validate the ISIC diagnosis AI algorithm (the winning diagnostic AI of that challenge).
classes_used: Pigmented actinic keratosis or intraepithelial carcinoma

use.training: Used to develop the ISIC diagnosis AI algorithm.
use.other: The ISIC AI algorithm was previously tested in a large online reader study using this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.method: One-sided equivalence (non-inferiority) test of correlated proportions
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess non-inferiority and equivalence between AI algorithms and clinicians for diagnosis and management decisions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Two-sided equivalence test of correlated proportions
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess equivalence in the management trial.
To estimate the precision of accuracy differences, sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics.
To evaluate diagnostic performance for each lesion category.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate diagnostic performance based on estimated probability of malignancy.
To calculate differences in accuracy between AI and clinicians.
Used as a secondary endpoint for comparison of clinician and AI performance.

hypothesis_testing.test: One-sided equivalence (non-inferiority) test of correlated proportions for the diagnostic trial; Two-sided equivalence test of correlated proportions for the management trial.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: Diagnostic trial: Pa - Pc ≤ –δ (AI is inferior to clinician by more than the equivalence margin δ). Management trial: There is a difference (non-equivalence) between the AI algorithm and the clinician.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Diagnostic trial: Pa - Pc > –δ (AI is not inferior to clinician). Superiority alternative: Pa - Pc ≥ δ (AI is superior to clinician). Management trial: There is equivalence (no difference) between the AI algorithm and the clinician.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Equivalence was declared if both one-sided null hypotheses were simultaneously rejected (for diagnostic trial) or if the 95% confidence intervals of the accuracy measures were entirely within the equivalence margin (–10% for diagnosis, –0.5% for management). Non-inferiority was claimed if the non-inferiority hypothesis was rejected. Superiority was claimed if the superiority hypothesis was rejected. The study found the 7-class AI diagnoses equivalent to specialists and significantly superior to novices, while the ISIC AI diagnoses were significantly inferior to specialists but superior to novices. For management, the 7-class AI was significantly inferior to specialists but showed mixed performance compared to novices.

Diagnostic Trial Cohort.description: 172 suspicious pigmented lesions from 124 patients. Primary endpoint: non-inferiority of correct categorical classification of seven diagnoses.
Diagnostic Trial Cohort.notes: The 7-class AI diagnoses were equivalent to specialists and significantly superior to novices. The ISIC AI diagnoses were significantly inferior to specialists but significantly superior to novices.

Management Trial Cohort.description: 5696 pigmented lesions from 66 high-risk patients. Primary endpoint: equivalence of management decisions (dismissal, biopsy, or monitoring).
metrics: Absolute accuracy difference in correct management decision
Management Trial Cohort.notes: The best 7-class management AI was significantly inferior to specialists' management in both scenarios. Compared with novices, the 7-class management AI was significantly inferior in scenario A but significantly superior in scenario B. The ISIC diagnosis management AI was significantly inferior to specialists.

architecture_used.main_model: Two mobile phone-powered AI algorithms: a new 7-class AI algorithm and the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) AI algorithm.
architecture_used.pretraining: The 7-class AI algorithm was developed using a proprietary database of expert dermoscopy images of skin lesions taken from mobile phones (MetaOptima Technology Database, 202,262 images). The ISIC AI algorithm was trained and validated using the ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Dermoscopy images captured via a mobile phone-powered dermoscopic attachment (MoleScope II). Specific pixel dimensions are not reported.

architecture_details.7_class_ai: A new 7-class diagnostic algorithm designed for pigmented skin lesions. Specific neural network architecture (e.g., CNN type, layers, heads) is not detailed in the provided text.
architecture_details.isic_ai: The winning diagnostic AI from the ISIC 2018 Challenge. Specific neural network architecture details are not provided in the text.

classification_or_output_head.7_class_ai: 7-class classification: Melanoma, Melanocytic naevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Pigmented actinic keratosis or intraepithelial carcinoma (AK/IEC), Benign keratotic lesion, Benign vascular lesion, Dermatofibroma.
classification_or_output_head.isic_ai: 7-class classification (same categories as above) and management decisions (dismissal, biopsy, or monitoring).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) AI algorithm
preprocessing_techniques: Image quality control and exclusion criteria: Lesions were excluded if they were mucosal, nail, or umbilical; amelanotic; had previous treatment; had insufficient image quality; had multiple obscuring bubbles or hair cover; were out-of-focus; or were incompletely imaged (outside the field of view).
Artifact reduction: Antiseptic gel or liquid was applied at the lesion-attachment interface to stop bright reflective artifacts.
Image capture parameters: Images were captured using a circular field of view (16 mm in diameter) via a mobile phone-powered dermoscopic attachment (MoleScope II) to mobile phones (iPhone 8 Plus).

Lesion size filtering: Lesions were assessable for imaging by AI when pigmented and bigger than 3 mm in the longest diameter in Vienna, or bigger than 4 mm (and >3 mm if chosen by the ground-truth clinician for excision or digital monitoring) in Sydney.
conclusions: The mobile phone-powered 7-class AI algorithm demonstrated diagnostic accuracy equivalent to specialist clinicians and significantly superior to novice clinicians for the diagnosis of pigmented skin cancer.
The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) AI algorithm was significantly inferior to specialist clinicians but significantly superior to novice clinicians in diagnostic accuracy.
The best 7-class management AI algorithm was significantly inferior to specialist clinicians for management decisions in both tested scenarios.

Compared to novice clinicians, the 7-class management AI was significantly inferior in scenario A (where monitoring is equivalent to dismissal) but significantly superior in scenario B (where monitoring is equivalent to biopsy of malignant lesions).
The ISIC diagnosis management AI algorithm was significantly inferior to specialist clinicians for management decisions.

An AI algorithm that performed superiorly in experimental reader studies (ISIC AI) was significantly inferior to specialists in a real-world clinical scenario, indicating that caution is needed when extrapolating experimental AI results to clinical practice.

Mobile phone-powered AI technology is simple, practical, and accurate for the diagnosis of suspicious pigmented skin cancer in a specialist setting, but its application for management decisions requires careful execution.
important_remarks: The study was funded by MetaOptima Technology, the company that developed the new 7-class AI algorithm, raising potential conflict of interest concerns.

The ISIC AI algorithm, which was superior to experts in a large experimental reader study, performed significantly inferior to specialists in this real-world clinical setting, highlighting a lack of reproducibility and caution needed when extrapolating experimental AI results to clinical practice.
The diagnostic trial cohort was limited to patients with modified Fitzpatrick I–III skin types, potentially limiting generalizability to darker skin types.

The management trial included only 18 malignant lesions out of 5696 total lesions, resulting in very low sensitivity for detecting malignancy (e.g., 55.6% for the best 7-class AI) and limiting statistical power for malignant outcomes.
The 7-class AI algorithm was trained on a proprietary database of 202,262 images, and specific neural network architecture details were not reported, limiting reproducibility.
The study was not randomized, which may introduce selection bias compared to randomized controlled trials.
There was a deviation in lesion size inclusion criteria between sites (3 mm in Vienna vs. 4 mm in Sydney), which was a logistical adjustment from the original protocol.

The 7-class management AI was significantly inferior to specialist clinicians for management decisions in both tested scenarios, indicating that simple translation from AI diagnosis to management is suboptimal.
Novice clinicians were defined as dermatology junior doctors or registrars in trainee positions, which may not represent the full spectrum of 'novice' users in broader clinical settings.
